data.title: Archer-Daniels Offers A Good Dividend Yield With Limited Downside.
data.description:
Introduction

With the recent broader market correction, the consumer staple index has emerged as an outperformer for the last couple of quarters. Although the returns aren’t much different between the broader market (SPY) and the consumer staple benchmark (XLP), the recent outperformance points toward investors’ growing favor toward the defensive segments of the US stock market.

Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (NYSE:ADM) is one of the large-cap laggards within the consumer staple sector, returning a negative 20% return over the last 12-month period.

The primary reason for this lag is attributed to the contraction in the crush margin as well as lower commodity prices. I believe this painful period can still continue going forward. However, cost-cutting initiatives should partially mitigate this pain. Moreover, a healthy dividend yield of 4.23% and limited downside potential in ADM are hard to ignore.

Archer Daniels is going through a painful period

Back in February, ADM reported somewhat downbeat results, with topline and bottom-line numbers falling short of analyst estimates. The revenue came in at $21.5 billion, missing the consensus by $1.27 billion, while non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.14, missing the consensus by $0.01.

The drop on the topline front was primarily driven by a fall in grain prices as well as somewhat slow demand in the Ag services and oilseeds business (AS&O). As ADM is primarily a trader of commodities like soybean, wheat and corn, the price fluctuation in these commodities tends to generally benefit companies like ADM. In periods of less volatile and falling prices, they generally witnessed declining profitability trends due to a glut in the market and inventory write-down effects.

This phenomenon can be witnessed in the graphs above. See how ADM’s operating profits witnessed a sudden spurt during the 2020-2022 periods while prices for all three primary commodities of ADM also went up. Currently, we are witnessing moderation of this trend with profits normalizing to lower levels as prices have retraced back to earlier levels.

That being said, about ADM’s trading business, the company also has several other businesses that are downstream to their trading business, like the crushing business and the carbohydrates solution and nutrition segment.

Similar to the headwinds in the trading business, the crushing business is also not faring well. The business is witnessing pressure on the soybean crushing margins due to increased use of used cooking oil (UCO) in the production of biodiesel and ample soybean harvest in South America. Due to the headwinds in both trading and crushing businesses, the Ag Services and Oils (AS&O) segment witnessed a ~32% YoY fall in operating profits for the 4Q24.

With respect to the carbohydrate solutions segment, the performance and dynamics are better. There has been a solid demand environment for sweeteners, starches and flour for the last few quarters and ethanol demand is also good, albeit margins are under pressure due to slight overproduction in ethanol. Overall the segment delivered ~3% operating profit growth for 4Q24 while operating profit remained flat YoY for FY2024.

Lastly, the nutrition segment, although considerably smaller than the other two segments, is far more profitable and a high-growth segment. The segment delivered ~10% operating profit decline for the year due to weak demand from a major client (Beyond Meat) and inflationary pressure on logistics and raw materials. However, long-term prospects for this segment are good, with growing demand for plant-based dairy snacks and specialized nutrients

That being said, about the dynamic for each of the segments, going forward, I believe ADM should continue to witness weakness in terms of operating profit growth, at least for the near term, led by lower crushing margins for soybeans. However, performance should gradually improve in the second half of FY25, primarily led by the cost-saving initiative. ADM’s management expects a $500-$750 million cost reduction within the next 3-5 years through various cost-cutting initiatives like logistic optimization, workforce reduction, and exiting low-margin businesses, etc. Management expects an anticipated $200-$300 million cost savings in 2025 itself. To put this into perspective, ADM’s operating profit for 2024 was ~$4.2 billion; a cost saving of $200-$300 million would mean ~5-7% of added profits from cost-saving efforts.

Archer Daniels’ dividend yield is improving

ADM has a long-standing history of dividend payment. According to Seeking Alpha quant ratings, the company gets a B- and an A+ grade for dividend safety and dividend consistency, respectively. Recently, the company increased its dividend by 2% to $0.51 after 4Q24 results. I believe there is a great deal of certainty with current dividend payouts given ADM’s long-standing 50 years of history of dividend payments and annual increases as well as a comfortable leverage ratio of ~2x.

In addition, I believe the company’s balance sheet position should be even better due to the cost-saving initiatives. These initiatives also include portfolio simplification efforts. In simple terms, ADM’s management would try to close down and divest certain businesses with less than the desired rate of return and profitability. As per management, they have already identified $2 billion worth of assets as of now. Once these initiatives are in place, they should result in better free cash flow for the company, which could then be used to either pay down debt or reward shareholders either in the form of higher dividend payouts or share buybacks.

The decline in stock price over the last few quarters and increased dividend payments have resulted in a steadily rising dividend yield of the stock. As of now, ADM has a forward dividend yield of 4.23%.

Valuation

According to Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, ADM’s valuation is rated as a B+, which is pretty decent in my opinion. The company is currently trading at 11.5x forward non-GAAP PE ratio as compared to the sector median of 16.3x.

While the stock looks cheap based on the PE ratio, I believe valuing ADM on a price-to-book basis would be a better gauge of valuation, as its business is linked to commodities, and profits tend to be high or low based on the prices of commodities and spreads prevalent in the market, which tends to distort the PE ratio. ADM’s current price-to-book ratio stands at ~1x, which means the current market cap of ADM is equal to the value of all its assets minus liabilities. For comparison, the sector median is ~2.6x and its last 5-year average is ~1.55x. Moreover, if we look at the historical price-to-book ratio of ADM, the current valuation stands close to the lower end of the range.

As can be seen in the graph above, this valuation metric dipped below 1 only on very few occasions, like the 2008 recession and the 2020 COVID crash. Moreover, the fall bottomed out near the 0.8x-0.9x price-to-book ratio. I believe the current situation of ADM is nowhere close to that of 2008 and 2020 for this ratio to fall to 0.8-0.9x levels. Hence, I believe there is limited downside for the stock. This, when combined with a healthy dividend yield of ~4.23%, I believe the stock is placed quite attractively at current valuations.

Risk

The AS&O segment is the primary profit-generating segment for ADM. This segment is heavily influenced by the prices of soybean and the crush spread associated with it. Currently, crush spreads are depressed due to higher use of cheaper imported used cooking oil (UCO). This trend could intensify and negatively impact the company’s profitability going forward. However, I believe the current valuation more than suffices for this risk.

Another risk is the trade war. Although tariffs by the US government are slightly positive for ADM, the real concern lies with respect to the retaliation from the trade partners. Retaliation, particularly in the agriculture space. Earlier during the first term of President Donald Trump, the retaliatory tariff by China on agricultural commodities led to a significant decrease in agricultural imports by China during that period. If ever retaliatory tariffs are raised against the US in the agricultural space, it could have a negative impact on the ADM.

Conclusion

ADM is currently going through a painful period with falling crush margins and lower profits from the trading business. I believe these worries have placed the company at quite an attractive valuation. The price-to-book ratio is near a historic low. Although there is no immediate catalyst for the company to turn around. I believe the 4.23% dividend yield and limited downside potential offer a good entry point in this company. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Penguin Solutions: A Cheap Stock With Real Growth And Real Risk
data.description:
Thesis

Penguin Solutions, Inc. (NASDAQ:PENG), which used to be called SMART Global Holdings, is a company based in the United States that supplies high-performance computing infrastructure, memory products, and LED technologies. Its main focus, it seems, is on supporting large-scale artificial intelligence systems for businesses. Since changing its name, the company has set itself up as what it calls a full-stack solutions provider. This means it now offers a mix of advanced hardware, software management, and support services, all bundled together. It does this across several key industries, technology, telecom, energy, and federal.

This identity shift is more than cosmetic, I believe it reflects a deliberate pivot toward long-term, value-added growth opportunities in AI infrastructure and next-gen memory architectures. My analysis argues that Penguin now finds itself at a pivotal inflection point where investor optimism fueled by a deeply discounted valuation and strategic momentum must contend with the reality of execution risks, cyclical demand variability, and structural margin constraints.

Valuation Meets Execution

When I evaluated the Q2 2025 earnings results and broader strategic positioning of Penguin, what I see is the emergence of a materially undervalued AI infrastructure platform company that’s finally beginning to hit its stride. The numbers are unambiguous: 28.33% year-over-year revenue growth, $20.86 million in top-line upside versus consensus, nearly doubling non-GAAP EPS, and a clear trajectory of operating leverage with margins expanding across the board.

But more important than the beat itself is what underpins it: execution across core growth areas, strategic customer expansion, and validation from industry partners.

Advanced Computing is where the thesis gets concrete. Mark Adams, Penguin’s CEO, was crystal clear on the call: “Our Q2 results demonstrate the progress we are making and the transformation of Penguin Solutions into a leader in high-performance, high-availability enterprise infrastructure solutions.”

This wasn’t lip service.

Advanced Computing clocked in at $200 million for the quarter, up 42% year-over-year and now more than half the company’s revenue. What matters here is not just the size of the business, but the quality of it. Penguin's integrating power, cooling, memory, networking, and compute into bespoke, scalable, and vendor-agnostic AI infrastructure environments. That’s a value-added play, not a volume game.

What particularly excites me is the expansion of Penguin’s software stack through the ICE ClusterWare AIM Service and the addition of multi-tenancy support. These features are essential capabilities that broaden the company’s addressable market from Fortune 500 enterprises to the booming cohort of Tier 2 cloud service providers, or as Adams referred to them, “neocloud” customers. These businesses often lack internal capabilities to manage complex AI cluster deployment, and Penguin’s full-stack, managed-service model is designed to plug that gap. During the call, Adams noted: “We found [neocloud] to be a very good market for opportunities for us,” and that the platform’s flexibility is already resonating with new types of buyers.

Diversified Demand Drivers

From a growth trajectory standpoint, analysts rightly honed in on the quality and sustainability of this demand. Denis Pyatchanin from Stifel pressed on how Penguin sees vertical exposure shifting into the second half. While Adams didn’t disclose specific customer names, he confirmed that sectors like federal, financial, and energy are expected to drive continued deployments. That’s critical. It implies that the growth narrative isn’t limited to hyperscalers, which tend to be lumpy, but is expanding across a broader base of enterprise and government customers.

Turning to Integrated Memory, the CXL momentum is another key bullish signal. In Q2, Penguin shipped $105 million in memory revenue, up 26% year-over-year. Importantly, the CXL add-in card has already been adopted by Dell as part of their server configuration offering, which is a major proof point. The implication here is powerful: Penguin is developing bleeding-edge memory solutions and it’s landing them in major OEM pipelines. On the call, Adams also emphasized that the partnership with SK hynix is evolving to address custom, low-volume markets not historically served by SK, which to me suggests incremental growth from underpenetrated niches.

Even in the LED segment, which has been structurally challenged, Penguin found ways to create value. Revenue stayed flat year over year at $60 million, but operating margins went up by nearly five percentage points. That was largely thanks to Penguin’s capital-light, outsourced model, along with a patent licensing deal with Daktronics (DAKT), which, incidentally, I wrote about last fall.

This demonstrates discipline.

No, Penguin isn’t relying on LED for growth. But it’s managing the business efficiently and monetizing its IP, which adds stability to the broader margin profile.

Margin Expansion Leverage

Operating leverage is finally starting to show up in a meaningful way too. Non-GAAP operating margin expanded to 13.4%, up 410 basis points from last year. Operating expenses declined both year-over-year and sequentially, even as the company ramped investment in R&D and go-to-market initiatives.

Importantly, this isn’t a company burning cash to chase growth. Cash and equivalents rose to $647 million, up $253 million sequentially, and net debt is now just $23 million. Operating cash flow swung to $73 million, and the company improved its cash conversion cycle by six days. In a market where capital discipline is increasingly rewarded, this matters.

Strategically, the partnerships with Dell (DELL)and SK Telecom (SKM) are giving Penguin legitimate go-to-market reach that far exceeds what a company its size typically commands. The fact that Dell’s global sales force is now offering Penguin’s AI software and managed services is no small development. Why? That distribution model alone could materially expand Penguin’s market access without requiring proportional investment in headcount or infrastructure.

So when you combine all of this, the earnings momentum, the expanding customer base, the operational discipline, and the emerging ecosystem partnerships, before we go deeper into the valuation and growth metrics, it's hard not to be bullish on Penguin. That said, the company raised full-year revenue guidance to 17% growth and bumped non-GAAP EPS guidance to $1.6, plus or minus $0.10, which tells me they’re seeing enough visibility in the pipeline to justify a more confident outlook.

Valuation & Growth Potential

With a forward P/E of 11.17 for fiscal 2025, dropping to 9.42 in 2026 and 7.36 by 2027, and an expected long-term EPS CAGR of 20%, the market is pricing this stock like a commoditized hardware vendor rather than a differentiated player in advanced computing, memory systems, and LED technologies.

Coupled with a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of just 5.7, versus a sector median of over 13, PENG appears significantly undervalued based on future cash flows and earnings power.

Despite a clear valuation disconnect that appears to favor PENG, I’d caution against treating this as a pure value layup. There’s nuance here, and if we take off the rose-colored lenses and weigh the risk side of the ledger, the picture becomes less linear. What looks cheap might in fact be deservedly so, at least in the near term.

Management’s own commentary during the Q2 2025 earnings call points directly to a structural issue that could stifle near-term upside: demand lumpiness. Mark Adams openly acknowledged, “We forecasted fiscal year '25 revenue to grow 15% at the midpoint... Due in large part to that shipment, we expect revenue and profits to be more first half weighted.”

Translation: don’t expect the second half to look like the first. This kind of front-loaded performance, while not unusual in enterprise hardware, introduces a level of volatility that complicates the earnings narrative, especially for a company trying to convince the market that it’s evolved beyond its cyclical roots.

So, the current valuation is cheap on paper, but if revenue slows sharply in the second half, the market may be less inclined to believe in that growth rate, regardless of what the out-year projections suggest. Samik Chatterjee from JPMorgan hit this point during the call, probing whether any economic uncertainty or AI capital efficiency recalibrations might affect enterprise and cloud spending. Adams' response: "It's pretty dynamic, to be honest with you" is honest, but lacking, and it does little to settle the question of predictability. We’re in an environment where capital efficiency is becoming a top-down mandate in many AI-driven organizations. That means customers are going to scrutinize ROI more aggressively, and that could elongate Penguin’s sales cycles or dilute deal sizes.

Hardware Margin Drag

Then there’s the margin story.

Q2’s 30.8% gross margin, while stable sequentially, declined 70 basis points year-over-year. The guidance for FY2025 is a 31% margin, plus or minus a point.

This isn’t catastrophic, but it’s telling.

The Advanced Computing segment, which now represents 55% of revenue, is still largely hardware-intensive. That means gross margins are inherently thinner compared to software or services-led revenue. Unless Penguin can significantly scale its software stack, such as ICE ClusterWare AIM or OriginAI, and grow post-deployment services to a much larger portion of the mix, margin upside may remain structurally capped. This is important because low-margin growth in itself doesn't guarantee operating leverage. Nate Olmstead, the CFO, was candid in noting that a higher hardware mix is dragging on the consolidated gross margin, even though services are growing.

Trade Policy Exposure

And then there’s something that hardly needs explaining anymore, especially not this early into the new administration, with April barely underway, and that’s Penguin’s ongoing tangle with global supply chain headaches and tariffs, which, just to keep things interesting, continue to make everything that much more complicated.

Someone asked me about the tariffs today, and the way I put it, more or less, was this: imagine analysts had almost finished a perfectly decent puzzle, and then Trump came along, swept it off the table, and now we’re all crouched on the floor trying to piece it back together from scratch.

As for PENG, the Optimized LED segment, while only 16% of revenue, remains tethered to Chinese manufacturing through its Huizhou facility. And though Adams pointed out that LED margins were “fantastic” this quarter, up nearly five percentage points, the backdrop remains fragile. “We’ve been in this environment with this business for some time,” he said, referencing tariffs. But that doesn’t change the underlying risk: any further escalation in U.S.–China trade tensions, or even a marginal shift in policy, could quickly compress margins. LED is no longer a growth vector, but it is a profit contributor, and if it gets hit by a tariff shock, that could chip away at one of the more stable puzzle pieces of the income statement.

Key Leadership Transition

Leadership continuity is another often underappreciated risk. Jack Pacheco, Penguin’s long-time EVP, COO, and President of Integrated Memory, is retiring at year-end. While transitions happen, this isn’t a routine departure. Pacheco has been instrumental in shaping Penguin’s memory business, which not only accounts for nearly a third of revenue but is also where some of the most promising future-facing work, such as CXL development and Optical Memory Appliance (OMA) architecture, is happening.

Losing institutional knowledge and executional oversight at this critical juncture could introduce latency into product development cycles or commercial traction just as customers begin evaluating next-gen memory solutions. Adams noted that Pacheco would help with the transition, but investors should watch closely for signs of leadership drag or organizational inertia.

Cash Flow Volatility

Finally, the backward-looking cash flow profile shows signs of friction. Levered free cash flow declined nearly 90% year-over-year, and while Q2’s $73 million in operating cash flow looks healthy, it’s not enough to fully erase the volatility of past quarters. The business is clearly transitioning, but free cash flow consistency remains elusive.

Sure, improvements in working capital, like getting inventory days down from 54 to 37, are a decent start. But let’s not get too carried away. All of this is still happening in a wider world where component lead times are stretched out like old chewing gum, and customers seem to be changing their minds on the fly. What that means, in practical terms, is that Penguin could still run into delays in booking revenue or sudden changes in when projects actually happen, none of which does any favors for keeping cash flow even remotely predictable.

Rating: Buy

Given today’s 10% drop, I’d still call PENG a “Buy.” The Q2 results show clear signs of momentum, revenue is up, margins are improving, and they’re generating cash, which is always nice. This all seems to come from solid progress in AI infrastructure and software integration. That said, there are real risks, demand remains uneven, and there’s a leadership transition to get through. Even so, the stock looks undervalued compared to how the business is shaping up, especially as it becomes more relevant in AI, cloud, and memory. I’m optimistic about the upside, but I’ll be keeping an eye on how well they actually pull it off.

data.title: Best Buy: Tariffs Further Shake This Struggling Retailer
data.description:
On a day that President Trump has dubbed “Liberation Day,” the new administration has released a sweeping set of reciprocal tariffs on nations across the globe, most notably China and the European Union. Americans, already beset by high inflation, are likely to see further price increases, especially on electronics that are largely manufactured in Asia.

Against this backdrop, a tough situation for Best Buy (NYSE:BBY) is likely to turn for the worse. The largest electronics specialty retailer in the U.S. has already seen its share price drop ~25% this year. In the wake of intensifying tariffs, we’re likely to see Best Buy’s sales suffer even more.

I last wrote a bearish note on Best Buy in November, when the stock was trading just shy of $90. Now, with sweeping tariffs taking effect and stacking on top of already weak comparable sales, I’m reiterating my sell rating on Best Buy.

It’s worth noting that the risks for Best Buy aren’t just restricted to tariffs alone, though this is the biggest red flag for the stock (which we’ll get into in the next section in more detail). In my view, the following are also sore spots for the company:

  • Best Buy is a clunky legacy business that will have a tough time competing against Amazon. Electronics and appliances are among the easiest types of purchases to make online, where products and prices are universally equal. These are typically large, pre-researched purchases where a number of retailers offer the same products (and typically, retailers like Amazon (AMZN) will offer period deals from time to time). In this environment, it's unclear whether Best Buy, with its large store format and heavy operating costs, can survive.
  • Tough macroeconomy is leading to longer replacement cycles. Even before tariffs kicked in, consumer spending was already slowing on the back of rampant inflation. As the quality of products like mobile phones and computers improves, consumers are increasingly holding on to their devices for longer, lengthening replacement cycles and slowing sales for retailers like Best Buy.
  • A more deal-oriented consumer may pressure margins. Further adding to profitability and cash flow worries, Best Buy has stated that it has noticed more customers becoming more deal-oriented (making promotional moments like Black Friday even more important for the company). On top of making for a lumpier sales trend, Best Buy may choose to invest more into promotions to drive improved sales, which may cut into its margins.
  • Dividends may be unsustainable. Many hold Best Buy for the ~4% yield, but at the moment the company is spending more on its shareholder returns program than it's bringing in for cash flow. If sales pressures continue, Best Buy may have to slow down its shareholder returns.

Though tempting to swoop in and buy Best Buy for its cheaper stock and its dividend on the recent dip, I’d resist that temptation and remain on the sidelines.

New tariffs will decimate low single digit growth plan for FY25

Before we discuss the potential impact of tariffs on Best Buy, let’s first get grounded on Best Buy’s operations and its outlook excluding tariffs. The company’s latest fiscal Q4 results are shown in the chart below:

Now, on paper, the company did moderately well, at least against depressed expectations, in Q4. Best Buy’s revenue declined -4.8% y/y to $13.95 billion, beating Wall Street’s much more dire expectations of $13.70 billion (-6.4% y/y) by a two point margin. Further, we note that Best Buy faced a tough comp from a 14-week quarter in Q4 of FY24. As such, we look to its comparable sales data (which measures sales growth from existing stores, excluding newly opened or closed locations) which has been adjusted for this anomaly.

Comparable sales growth of 0.2% in the domestic segment and 3.8% in the international segment accelerated rather sharply from a -2.8% and -3.7% decline in Q3, respectively. Here, however; we have to take caution. The adjustment for a prior-year 14 week quarter is an entirely discretionary calculation that companies can take liberties with (for example, companies can choose to eliminate 1/14th of revenue from the prior year, or shift actual calendar week sales to get the “what if” figure if the quarter only had 13 weeks instead of 14). In other words, it’s likely that Best Buy chose its comparable sales adjustment for the prior-year 14th week to make its sales appear in the best light possible, which may be partially responsible for the sequential improvement versus Q3.

Now we move onto FY26, which for Best Buy is the current fiscal year ending in January 2026. The company’s baseline expectation, assuming no further tariffs beyond those that were already known in China/Mexico/Canada, called for 0-2% comparable sales growth, adjusted operating margins of 4.2-4.4% (versus 4.2% in FY25), and $6.20-$6.60 in pro forma EPS (paltry 0.5% growth at the midpoint versus $6.37 in FY25).

The company’s guidance comes with the important caveat that it doesn’t include the impacts of any additional tariffs. Of course, Trump’s Rose Garden speech on April 2 has quashed hopes that tariffs will remain at the 10% baseline levels.

Previously, on the Q4 earnings call, CEO Corie Barry noted that a 10% baseline tariff on China remaining in place through the remainder of FY26 would have up to a -1 point impact on comparable sales growth in FY26:

The fiscal 2026 guidance we provided this morning does not include the impact of the recently enacted tariffs. This is because it is a highly dynamic situation with uncertainty about the duration, timing, amount, and countries involved, in addition to the potential action of others in the industry, as well as the potential reaction of American consumers. That being said, we believe it is helpful to provide some level of context. Based on our early analysis, if the China tariffs that went into effect on February 4th remain at the 10% level for the full year, we believe they would have a negative impact in the ballpark of 1 point of comparable sales. This would mainly impact quarters two through four.

I want to stress that our deeply tenured and talented teams are experienced at operating in volatile conditions and this is also a situation where our partnerships with vendors are extremely valuable. We intend to execute the strategic plan we set for this year while navigating the tariff environment.”

The latest tariff schedule pegs China’s new tariff rate at 54%, far above the previous 10% baseline. On top of this, the president enacted a largely unexpected 20% tariff on the European Union, alongside a 46% tariff on Vietnam and similar rates on other important countries within the Southeast Asia supply chain.

We can’t try to predict the impact of these tariffs on Best Buy’s P&L for the year, any more than Best Buy itself can with any degree of precision. However, we can draw a smart inference that if the company was expecting a -1 point impact at a baseline 10% China tariff rate, a 54% tariff will have much sharper impacts, not to mention the other countries that got added to the tariff plan.

We should also note that part of Best Buy’s Q4 strength in returning to positive comparable sales comps could be a head fake driven by consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs (the same way Democratic success in federal elections tends to produce a temporary spike in firearm sales). Again, we emphasize that Best Buy’s bread and butter lies in large, well-researched and long-planned purchases of things like phones, computers, and heavyweight home appliances - inflation-weary consumers could have stocked up on their needs during the holiday season, knowing that there was a risk of further price increases.

We should remark here that Best Buy’s margin was already in a precarious position. In Q4, gross margin slid to a mere 20.9%, up 40bps y/y - sequentially much weaker than 23.5% in Q3, which had improved 60bps y/y. The major theme over the past year has been that consumers have been requiring incentives to trigger purchases, and Best Buy has followed its competitors into a very promotional holiday sales environment.

Amid the threat of tariffs and manufacturers passing on costs to consumers via higher sticker prices, Best Buy may face pressure to sacrifice its margins further to deploy more frequent/deeper discounts to avoid losing too much market share.

All of this, of course, is bad news for Best Buy’s dividend. In FY25, the company generated $2.1 billion of operating cash flow and spent $0.7 billion on capex, or $1.4 billion in free cash flow. Meanwhile, it spent $1.3 billion on combined dividends and share repurchases - in other words, spending nearly the entirety of its cash flow on its shareholder returns program.

Best Buy’s FY26 guidance calls for a slight step-up in capex to a $700-750 million range, and with the impact of tariffs we can likely say goodbye to the prospect of comparable sales and positive earnings growth. I’d be wary of a step-down in the company’s shareholder returns program in FY26.

Key takeaways

Even before new tariffs kicked in, Best Buy was already dealing with a weak sales environment, with AI innovations not yielding the expected upgrade rates to PCs as initially expected (which, incidentally, was a sentiment echoed by the likes of Western Digital (WDC) and Micron (MU) as the reason for weaker NAND storage sales).

Tariffs, then, are a double whammy on a business that is already dealing with extreme macro challenges. We note that Best Buy trades at just a ~10x P/E multiple against the $6.40 midpoint of its current guidance, and a cheap valuation/rich dividend are the main “upside risks” that I see to this stock. But at the same time, I expect management to slash its expectations, and possibly its dividend, as the company filters in new expectations for the post-tariff world.

In my assessment, there’s too much risk and too little reward for this name.

data.title: Charter: An Attractive Safe Haven Amidst Macro Uncertainty (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications (NASDAQ:CHTR) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining about 35%, as it manages through cord cutting while also engaging in M&A to simplify its capital structure. I last covered shares in July, rating them a “hold,” and like the market, the stock has been flat since then, validating the rating. With its US service business, Charter is fairly immune from tariffs with just a modest potential impact on maintenance costs, though a weaker economy could accelerate cord-cutting. With its business fairly insulated from trade pressures, now is a good time to see if CHTR is an attractive safe haven. I am bullish.

Since last covering Charter, the company announced a meaningful acquisition, which will improve its financial profile, while not having any operational risk. In November, Charter agreed to acquire Liberty Broadband (LBRDA). This deal has received shareholder approval. It aims to close it on June 30, 2027, an incredibly long-time away in M&A. It is an all-cash deal, and Liberty shareholders will receive 0.236 shares in CHTR for each share they own. Liberty Broadband has essentially two assets: 45.6 million shares of Charter and GCI, an Alaska communications company. GCI will be spun-out before closing.

Charter will issue 34 million shares to take out Liberty. This will reduce the share count by 11.6 million shares, worth about $4.4 billion at $375, current market values. CHTR will also assume ~$2.6 billion of Liberty debt, $180 million of Liberty preferred stock, and up to $420 million of tax liability associated with the GCI spin-out. That is about $3.2 billion of liabilities, netting CHTR savings of $1.2 billion. So long as the stock stays above $275, this deal will be accretive to CHTR. The current benefit is about $7 a share.

This is a win for Charter, and it is highly likely the deal is accretive. For Liberty, it removes one more tracking stock from the complex and will improve liquidity of shareholders as they get CHTR stock. Given the need to spin-out GCI, it will take time to close, but since the deal is really just a share swap, there are no operations to integrate, so I do not see this being a distraction for management. There will be no regulatory issues.

At the same time, Charter has performed broadly in keeping with my expectations on the revenue side, though its expense discipline has exceeded my baseline. Indeed when it last reported earnings on January 31st, Charter smashed expectations, earning $10.32, which was $1.10 ahead of consensus. In 2024, Charter grew revenue by 1% to $55.1 billion while increasing EBITDA 3% to $22.6 billion. In Q4 specifically, residential revenue fell by 0.4% to $10.7 billion while commercial grew by 1.9% to $1.8 billion. Expenses were up just 0.3%, enabling 3.4% EBITDA growth to $5.8 billion.

As you can see below, Charter continues to see attrition in its legacy products while reporting excellent growth in its mobile offering. I was encouraged to see video trends improve from their early-2024 levels while internet activity has worsened. One challenge for the cable companies is that home sales have been quite low. This churn creates a natural opportunity to win new customers. Given where rates are and weak affordability, I expect home sales to remain sluggish.

In aggregate, CHTR has 31.5 million total customer relationships, down 2%. Offsetting this, its $121.40 average residential bill was up 1.7%. So long as CHTR can keep declines inside of 2%, it should be able to maintain revenue and margins. We see the benefits of pricing at the segment levels. Internet subscribers were down 1.7% while video was down 8.7%. However, internet revenue rose by 0.9%, video fell by a more modest 7.4%.

I was particularly encouraged by what we are seeing in mobile. The company continues to grow ferociously. It is up to 9.9 million mobile lines from 7.8 million last year. Charter has been highly promotional in its effort to win mobile customers, often offering a free line for a year to existing internet/TV users. As a result, it was growing its customer count much more quickly than its revenue. That is now changing. Mobile revenue rose by 37%, 10% faster than its customer count.

CHTR is now earning revenue from more of its customer base, and it is proving an ability to retain customers even after they flip from promotional pricing. I expect mobile revenue to continue to grow faster than subscribers and rise at least 20% in 2025, helping to offset some lost video revenue. Given video declined, we do see CHTR having fewer 3 product customers, which are the most valuable. However with mobile continuing to grow, I am hopeful that the company can retain more customers in the two product tier.

Charter remains cash flow generative, with $4.3 billion of free cash flow in 2024. It generally uses cash flow to buy back stock, but it did just $113 million of buybacks in Q4 as it had to pause repurchases until after the vote on the Liberty deal; it can now repurchase stock. Still, its share count was down 3% from last year. It has 4.13x debt/EBITDA leverage, and 4.24x including the pending Liberty Broadband deal. The company targets 4-4.5x leverage, so I expect it to now direct free cash flow to repurchases.

The other key variable for the company is its capital spending. It has been ramping up cap-ex to expand its network and take advantage of rural subsidies. It expects that 2025 will be the year of peak capital spending, with $12 billion of investments expected. After that, we should see capital spending gradually decline, which will help preserve free cash flow even if we see some revenue declines. I do see a risk that long-run cap-ex runs closer to $9 billion than $8 billion, given potential input cost inflation over time.

Last July, I was targeting $2.4-$2.6 billion of 2025 free cash flow, flexing up to $3.4 billion in 2026. I expect flattish revenue growth in 2025. Relative to 2024, we are likely to have ~$1.5-1.8 billion of headwinds from increased cap-ex, refinancing debt at higher rates, and working capital. That leads to a baseline 2025 free cash flow of $2.6-2.8 billion. Assuming 1% faster EBITDA growth than revenue growth, given cost control efforts, I would expect ~$2.8 billion of 2025 free cash flow, with free cash flow likely to be ~$3.8 billion in 2026, given lower cap-ex.

Assuming $9 billion of long-term cap-ex, CHTR should be generating at least $5-5.5 billion of free cash flow over time, or about $36 a share. At an 8% long-term free cash flow yield given growth prospects are minimal, shares have an underlying value of $450. I would remove ~$4 billion of value, for the free cash flow shortfall over the next two years but add about $7 of accretion from the Liberty deal for a fair value of $430.

That leaves shares with 15% upside. I view this as an attractive opportunity, especially with CHTR, a stock that is fairly safe from macro noise, given its US focus. With its cap-ex and cost-cutting plan progressing credibly and its domestic focus, CHTR shares can outperform during this period of uncertainty, and I am upgrading Charter to a buy.

data.title: LegalZoom: A Horrible Macro Backdrop For LZ To Thrive (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about LegalZoom (LZ) previously (16th December 2024) with a buy rating as I expected LZ to start printing better growth performance moving forward. My price target was set at ~$11.7 when the stock was at $8.07 previously. While LZ share price did not hit my price target, it did reach close enough at ~$10.5, which was a 30% gain. That said, I think the recent macro development and fundamentals have made me cautious over the near term. As such, I downgrade LZ to a hold rating.

Why I’m Cautious in the Near Term

The first red flag for me is the deterioration in average revenue per unit [ARPU]. ARPU fell 5% y/y to $263 in 4Q24, down from $277 in 4Q23 and $264 in 3Q24. Although management said that the decline was due to pricing shifts in compliance-related and virtual mail subscriptions, which may be true, the reality is that LZ is adding more customers who are spending less. Although this is still positive growth on an absolute basis, a 500 bps decline in ARPU is huge and severely limits the ability for LZ to print accelerating growth (which was my expectation). Another way to look at how impactful this is, is that subscription revenue only grew 2% despite subscription unit growth of 11%.

But perhaps the bigger concern I have is at the macro level. With the US government now rolling out sweeping tariffs, I think there is substantial risk that business formation activity will plummet as SMEs are likely to delay their plans until they are able to resolve the implied cost headwinds from the tariffs. Specifically, here is how I view the tariffs potentially hurting LZ. As a recap, LZ depends heavily on business formation (i.e., new business starts) to grow. While Trump's intention is to protect US manufacturing, which may be true over time, I believe the immediate impact is higher input costs, which immediately hurt businesses. Then, there is the potential for retaliatory threats from key partners like Europe and China, which significantly raises the uncertainty about supply chain stability, which leads to hesitation to invest or start new businesses. I am not the only one that has this concern; the Fed has also flagged this as a headwind to growth.

Even if all these tariffs are just means for Trump to make a deal, I think the damage has been done—uncertainty around trade and input cost, business sentiment, and consumer confidence. If formation activity sees a sharp decline because of this, this will be a real headwind for LZ’s growth. It also does not help that LZ made the decision to pull back both the LZ Tax product and BOIR-related services, which will result in a 400 bps drag on FY25 revenue growth. As much as I like to agree with management that this is a strategic shift, in light of the macro developments and poor ARPU trend, this is a relatively negative development because it removes the monetization lever that LZ has.

Touching a little on the Formation Nation acquisition, which I believe many saw as a positive, I think it is still early to judge whether this is a successful add to the business. For one, we don’t really know how revenue accretive this acquisition is (management only notes EBITDA contribution in FY25). More importantly, the acquired businesses—specifically, Inc Authority and NCH—business models are currently transactional. Remember that LZ is undergoing the transition from transactional to subscription. Although LZ has seen success so far, this shift still presents meaningful execution risk. If not executed well, this acquisition could be value destructive for the business. At a high level, this, layered with the already soft ARPU trends and macro concern, makes the growth outlook a lot murkier.

Valuation

If you refer to my previous model, there were three main assumptions that I had made: (1) revenue accelerates to high single digits by FY26; (2) EBITDA margin expands to 25% in FY25 and stays flat in FY26; and (3) forward EBITDA multiple is at 10x. With my more bearish view, as discussed above, I cannot justify any of these three assumptions anymore. My revised view is that growth is very likely going to stay in the low single digits (FY24 growth was just 3.2% y/y); EBITDA margin is not going to expand to 25% in FY25; and multiples have no reason to trade at 10x with this lackluster growth outlook. It is likely that LZ valuation continues to trade at 8x. Using these assumptions, LZ share price is worth ~$7.70 based on my model, and this does not justify a buy rating.

Conclusion

I downgrade LZ to a hold rating for LZ. I think the next few quarters are going to be really tough and uncertain for the business. The macro environment has become a lot worse than December, and I am not bullish about business formation activities moving forward. Without a clear catalyst for revenue growth, I don’t see the risk/reward justifying a buy at this stage. I’ll revisit if signs emerge that monetization is stabilizing and the macro dust settles. For now, I’m staying on the sidelines.

data.title: Japan Megabank Stocks Plunge As Trump Tariffs Risk Monetary Policy Normalization
data.description:
Shares in Japan's megabanks came under pressure on April 3 after US President Donald Trump imposed higher-than-expected tariffs, raising fears that expected interest rate hikes will be delayed.

The stock price of Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group Inc. plunged 7.1% on April 3 trading, while Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group Inc.'s (SMFG) was down 7.6% and Mizuho Financial Group Inc.'s (MFG) fell 7.9% — all higher on day losses than the 2.7% drop in the Nikkei Average Index. A banking stock index also fell, dropping 7.2%.

Japan's lenders, particularly the megabanks, had hoped expected interest rate rises would boost net interest margins. The Trump administration tariffs have cast those rate hikes into question.

"No way. That will never happen," Hiroshi Kubotani, a senior economist at NLI Research Institute, said about expectations that the Bank of Japan (BOJ) may raise interest rates in July. This delay "should be the reason" for the fall in megabanks' stocks, Kubotani said.

Trump tariffs

The Trump administration announced that the US will impose a 24% reciprocal tariff on Japanese goods, effective April 9, while the US is set to impose a 25% levy on Japan-made automobiles bound for the US market. The tariff salvo is fueling concern that the Japanese economy will slow, forcing the central bank to be more cautious over interest rates.

The BOJ raised its policy rate to 0.5% from 0.25% in January, the third hike since it moved away from negative interest rates in March 2024. The central bank's benchmark interest rate is now at its highest level since 2008, and many economists had expected it to rise to 1.0% by the end of 2025 as part of monetary policy normalization.

BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda previously indicated that tariffs would play a part in the central bank's approach. "Once the [US] tariff policy is detailed, we will put it into consideration for our policy," Ueda said at a press conference following the Jan. 24 policy meeting.

"The drop in the major bank stocks today is partly profit-taking after their outperformance in the first quarter, and possibly reflecting smaller probability of policy rate hikes after the larger-than-anticipated Trump tariffs which may result in a slowdown of both the US and Japanese economies," Michael Makdad, a senior analyst at Morningstar, told S&P Global Market Intelligence.

The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development downgraded its forecast for Japan's gross domestic growth to 1.1% in 2025 in March, 0.4 percentage points lower than its December estimate. That compares with a 0.1% GDP growth in 2024. The Mitsubishi Research Institute on Mar. 11 also estimated Japan's economy would grow 1.1% in 2025.

"Now, I'm pessimistic the estimated growth could not be reached," Yuta Asai, an economist at Mitsubishi Research Institute, told Market Intelligence after the tariff announcement. "A combination of the 24% reciprocal tariff with 25% auto tariff will put a drag on the Japanese economy. Given that, the BOJ won't raise rates higher and higher," Asai added.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Washington Trust: The Macroeconomic Context Is No Longer Favorable
data.description:
In my last article on Washington Trust Bancorp (NASDAQ:WASH) at the end of October 2024, I considered it a buy because I believed that the new macroeconomic context could favor it. At the time, GDP growth was not a problem, and it could also benefit from a more accommodating Fed than expected.

Months later, a lot has changed, and the introduction of tariffs is threatening the country's economic growth. Furthermore, economists believe that tariffs could lead to an acceleration of inflation, and this will prevent the Fed from reducing rates. So, a resurgence of inflation and a slowdown in economic growth is a combination that does not bode well for a cyclical business such as banking.

However, WASH has proven that it can maintain a constant dividend even in the worst phases of the economic cycle, and its Tangible Book Value is priced below the historical average. For these reasons, I have preferred a hold rating rather than a sell, but in the event of a recession, I think the price per share will suffer even though the stock is already quite cheap.

What to expect in 2025

As mentioned, the macroeconomic context is deteriorating rapidly, and the future looks very uncertain. The problem for WASH is that its room for maneuver is rather limited since its deposits are declining and are quite expensive. All its problems stem from this.

Non-interest-bearing deposits from 2023 to 2024 have shrunk despite the fact that interest rates have decreased, and this is already the first negative sign; many peers have already experienced a turnaround from this point of view. Furthermore, time deposits are still an important component of total deposits, accounting for 29%. There has certainly been an improvement compared to 33% in 2023, but it is not enough to significantly reduce the cost of deposits.

2025 is an important year because $1.28 billion of time deposits (almost all of them) will expire, and their weighted average rate is 4.25%. The aim will be to refinance them at a lower rate, but what is happening these days makes me doubt it.

The tariffs imposed by Trump are the highest in more than 100 years, and there is a serious risk that inflation will rise again. The Fed may not be able to reduce interest rates; on the contrary, it may be forced to increase them. At that point, it will be difficult to refinance time deposits at a more advantageous rate, and WASH's NIM would continue to suffer. Just think that the December spot margin was only 2.07%.

Management expects it to increase to 2.45%-2.50% by Q4 2024 thanks to a repositioning plan that began a few quarters ago. Low-cost deposits are struggling to increase, and this is a problem given that the loan-to-deposit ratio is around 105%. The lack of deposit growth is blocking the gears, which is why management has decided to sell part of its loans and securities portfolio to release liquidity. With the proceeds, it will repay the more expensive deposits and borrowings, as well as reinvest in assets with a higher return than those sold. This maneuver should increase the NIM, but in the short term it generated a loss in Q4 2024. At the moment the bank is cheap, but instead of doing buybacks it is issuing capital ($70.50 million) in Q4 2024 to finance the repositioning plan. So beware of dilution.

This strategy is also having an impact on the composition of loans, as management is getting rid of residential real estate loans. Over 75% of the volumes generated are sold and have also been removed from the portfolio.

As you can see, they have suffered a sharp decline over the last year, but $345 million has been reclassified in the Held-for-Sale section. It is not clear to me why management decided to remove only these loans and leave the CRE loans unchanged, now with an even greater weight within the portfolio. I suppose it is a question of demand and management will continue along these lines in the future.

At the moment, the focus seems to be mainly on the origination of C&I loans, since new deposits are more likely to come from these:

We are still out looking at real estate deals. We're seeing opportunity. The pricing is decent. The structure is good. So, we're calibrating the growth there, wanting to make loans, wanting to, again, focus on C&I because it tends to bring more deposits with it. Our priority is on the funding side of things and making sure we fund loan growth appropriately.

Ned Handy - Chairman & Chief Executive Officer

So, it's clear that we always come back to the starting point: everything else derives from the growth of deposits, and therefore that is the key element to improve.

Dividend sustainability

From a macroeconomic point of view, it doesn't seem like a good time to invest in a cyclical business like banking, but WASH undoubtedly has some features in its favor. The dividend yield has shot up to 8% after the recent collapse.

It has been paying a steady/growing dividend for 31 years in a row, and it did so even during the GFC and the pandemic.

In the long term, EPS tends to grow and cover the dividend. This has not been the case in the last year, but I have explained why: the sale of the securities materialized the losses and impacted the income statement. However, this is an extraordinary loss and will not happen again. It was necessary to strengthen the balance sheet.

Overall, I don't expect the dividend to be cut anytime soon, even if the economic environment worsens. However, I have serious doubts about its growth.

The 10-year CAGR is 5.84%, not bad, but the dividend hasn't been increased since 2023 and I don't see any room for growth in the coming quarters. Receiving an 8% dividend yield is probably already enough, but for those with a long-term view, the yield on cost isn't very appealing.

Conclusion

WASH is a bank that is going through a difficult period due to weak deposit growth. The NIM is very low, but the reclassification plan could lead to an improvement of up to 47 basis points in 2025. The dividend seems solid, but I don't expect growth in the coming quarters.

Tangible book value per share is well below the 10-year average, which may imply undervaluation, but I would be cautious given that the probability of recession is increasing due to tariffs. The risk is a contraction of international trade, so at least for the moment I would avoid cyclical businesses, regardless of their current valuation.

data.title: Consumer Goods Most Affected By Reciprocal Tariffs Announced By U.S. Administration
data.description:
Tariffs for all: 10 percentage points now, 16.8 percentage points to follow

The US government has declared an International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) action, citing "a lack of reciprocity in our bilateral trade relationships, disparate tariff rates and non-tariff barriers, and U.S. trading partners' economic policies that suppress domestic wages and consumption."

As a consequence, tariffs of 10 percentage points will be applied to all countries and products (with several exceptions) from April 5 with additional country-specific rates to be applied from April 9.

Functionally speaking, exceptions will include all imports from Canada and Mexico as well as products already subject to, or under investigation for, duties under other IEEPA measures or Section 232 (national security) tariff provisions.

The calculation of the country-specific duty rates includes differential tariff rates, non-tariff barriers, currency practices and the use of value-added taxes.

  • No details are provided on how precisely these have been incorporated for each country, although our prior analysis suggests around 4.8 percentage points of increase could be the result of differential tariff rates alone.
  • The additional country duty rates vary from 0 percentage point (including the UK, Brazil and Singapore) to as much as 40 percentage points (including Lesotho at 40 percentage points, Cambodia at 39 percentage points, Vietnam at 36 percentage points, Thailand at 26 percentage points and mainland China at 24 percentage points). The impact on individual countries will depend on the share of their exports to the US, typically in the 20%-30% range.
  • Taking a weighted average, that implies the average tariff rate for the countries and products covered under the new duties is 26.8%, implying a 16.8-percentage-point rise on April 9.
  • The implied increase on all US imports — i.e., when adding back Canada and Mexico — is equivalent to 20.5 percentage points, adding US$458 billion of import duties to be paid by US buyers of overseas goods and services.

Unhappy holidays: Consumer goods face brunt of tariffs

The impact of the new duties on individual supply chains can be calculated from the share of imports by country and the new duty rates applied — i.e., those products with a high proportion of imports from higher-tariff-rate countries will experience a higher rate.

Finished consumer goods are among the most notable examples as a result. Imports of toys and video games, which are predominantly imported from mainland China, face an average duty rate increase of 30.4 percentage points. That comes on top of the 20 percentage points' higher duty rates applied against imports from mainland China.

Other examples include computer parts (29.6 percentage points), clothing (28.3 percentage points, with some as high as 36.9 percentage points) and smartphones (27.0 percentage points).

Sectors where supply chains already relocated to Canada or Mexico face lower additional rates, including aerospace parts (10.6 percentage points), televisions (9.6 percentage points) and medical diagnostic equipment (9.5 percentage points). The food and spirits sectors are also relatively unaffected.

It should be noted that imports from Canada and Mexico that are not United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) free trade-eligible will still pay an additional 25% rate under previously announced rules.

Many of the products that are excluded from these new duties will face their own tariff reviews and may end up with duties of around 25%, including copper, lumber, pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, after the reviews are completed in a few months.

Autos as well as steel and aluminum already have 25% duties applied.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CIO Notebook: Risk Assets Under Pressure As Trump Expands Tariffs
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

Despite short-term uncertainty, we believe that the U.S. will avoid a meaningful and protracted economic deceleration and that tariffs and trade policy changes may stimulate growth in Europe and Asia, narrowing valuation and earnings gaps.

President Donald J. Trump declared a national economic emergency yesterday as the basis for a slate of sweeping global tariffs set to begin over the next week. He cited “large and persistent annual U.S. good trades deficits” which have led to a “hollowing out of our manufacturing base” as rationale in his announcement.

Through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (“IEEPA”), the President announced a baseline 10% tariff rate for all imports from any destination country to go into effect on April 5th. He also announced meaningfully higher tariffs for sixty of the “worst offenders,” including tariffs of 34% on China (on top of the 20% levy already announced), 20% on the European Union, 32% on Taiwan, 25% on South Korea, 46% on Vietnam, and 49% on Cambodia. These reciprocal tariffs are set to go into effect on April 9th.

Notable exclusions to the reciprocal tariffs are steel, aluminum, autos and auto parts, copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, bullion, and energy and certain minerals that are not available domestically. In addition, the current 25% proposed tariffs on Canada and Mexico remain in place, but nothing more has been announced for those two countries. The implication is that USMCA-compliant goods will not be subject to any tariffs, while non-USMCA compliant goods and non-USMCA compliance energy will be subject to 25% and 10%, respectively.

Equity, bond, currency, and commodity markets globally are reacting to the news, with U.S. equities down meaningfully to open the trading day. U.S. growth stocks and small cap names are faring the worst in early trading, while the S&P 500 is facing its sharpest decline in two years. European and Asian equities also traded lower on the news, but were down less than their U.S. counterparts. Yields are also moving lower, as the 10-Year Treasury now sits just above 4% -- well off its 2025 high of 4.8%. The VIX, often used as a measure of volatility for the U.S. market, surged over +20% following the announcement, and while gold was down modestly, it breeched an all-time high in yesterday’s session; the dollar is also off more than -2%.

Concerns center on the impact of higher prices on both consumers and businesses. U.S. consumers have little appetite or ability to withstand another round of price increases so quickly following the recent bout of inflation. Businesses, therefore, may struggle to pass the price increases through their supply chains and/or to end consumers, which may weigh on margins and the anticipated acceleration of earnings in 2025. Even if tariffs are negotiated lower or removed – the White House statement noted that the President could roll back the tariffs if he believes the threat is “satisfied, resolved, or mitigated” – the overhang of uncertainty is unlikely to lift fully in the near future; this points to an inflection of economic activity likely delayed to the back half of year if more clarity is delivered.

What remains up for debate as it relates to the President’s actions is if this is part of an aggressive approach to engender concessions from trading partners or if it is indicative of a broader, longer-term plan to generate meaningful revenue to offset the spending necessary to deliver on Trump’s tax promises while also shrinking the budget deficit. Our view remains that the reciprocal tariffs are likely to result in negotiation with some of our trading partners and the more specific actions already announced for Canada and Mexico, with no further escalation today, point to a goal of renegotiating the USMCA well ahead of its 2026 planned review.

Post-press conference comments from U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent pointed to the announced tariff rates as the “high end,” provided that countries do not retaliate. He also noted that his focus, and that of Congressional Republicans, is now squarely on passing the tax bill, which includes not only the extension of tax cuts within the 2017 Tax Cuts & Jobs Act (“TCJA”), but also potentially a suspension of taxes on tips and Social Security payments as well as the deductibility of interest paid on auto loans for purchases of American made autos.

While tariff revenues will certainly be a welcome addition to the assets side of the balance sheet, we believe that maximizing that revenue source is unlikely to be the primary driver for the Trump Administration. We are also encouraged by the shift of attention to addressing both the looming debt ceiling breach and the expiring tax cuts, as this reflects one of the key platform promises that is likely to cushion the sentiment blow inflicted by today’s announcement. Evidence of meaningful progress in cementing the pillars of the TCJA and demonstrating a concerted effort to tackle the burgeoning budget deficit could help create a ceiling on long-term yields which would be beneficial for borrowers across the U.S. economy – not least of which is the U.S. government.

However, we would be remiss not to acknowledge that the level of uncertainty, at least in the short-term, has been raised by these actions. Retaliation is likely, particularly from the “worst offenders.” There are likely to be countries which take issue with the Trump Administration’s calculation of “tariff charged on the U.S.” given that this was based on the trade balance versus the actual levies on U.S. goods – and therefore is difficult to counter in the short to mid-term. Recession risk and inflation expectations are likely to increase modestly and the combination of potentially slower growth and higher prices will once again shift attention to the Fed and the difficult position it may be in should tariffs of this magnitude remain in place for an extended period.

Despite this overhang, we believe that the U.S. will avoid a meaningful and protracted economic deceleration. We expect uncertainty to ease as we move closer to the second half of the year and believe a sustainable rotation away from 2023 to 2024 equity market leaders is underway as U.S. trade policy changes encourage growth stimulus in Europe and Asia, narrowing gaps in earnings growth and valuation. We maintain a balanced view on asset allocation, focusing on diversification across regions, sectors, and styles to manage risks from both inflation and growth slowdowns.

Important Information:

This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types. Unless otherwise indicated, returns shown reflect reinvestment of dividends and distributions. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Portfolio positioning views expressed herein are those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group, which may include those of the Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee. Asset allocation and positioning views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. data.title: Axos Financial: A Very Well Managed Bank
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Axos Financial Inc (NYSE:AX) is a regional U.S. bank operating nationwide, headquartered in Las Vegas, Nevada. AX offers a broad range of loan products such as residential mortgages, commercial loans, auto loans and consumer loans. The bank also provides wealth management services. The bank has delivered strong net interest margins while achieving a modest nonperforming loans to total loans ratio with an appropriate amount of leverage for the bank’s risk profile. When we consider overhead expenses, AX manages overhead expenses well compared to its competitors with an above-average efficiency ratio. Furthermore, the bank has outperformed its competitors in terms of return on assets and return on equity, illustrating AX’s proficiency to propel shareholders' equity, making the bank more valuable over time. The valuation implies an estimated five-year annual return of 18.0%, making AX a buy.

Net Interest Margin

Axos Financial has reached an updated median NIM throughout the previous decade of 4.2%.

While contrasting AX’s ten-year median NIM in comparison to its key competitors using the most current quarterly data reveals that Customers Bancorp (CUBI), First Interstate BancSystem (FIBK) and Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) each possess a ten-year median NIM of 3.0%, 3.4%, and 2.7%, respectively. This ranks AX as possessing the highest decade-long median NIM.

If we turn our focus to the near-term NIM trend, it can be seen that Axos Financial achieved a net interest margin of 4.9% in the quarter just concluded versus 4.3% in the corresponding quarter last year. This equates to a YoY growth rate of 15.8%, as opposed to CUBI, FIBK, and ASB, which reported YoY NIM growth of -5.8%, -2.5% and -2.6%, respectively. This ranks AX as realizing the highest NIM growth, therefore outperforming competing banks.

In the case of AX, the ten-year standard deviation of NIM is 0.00338, which ranks it as having the third lowest NIM standard deviation amongst its peers. Therefore, in my opinion, this shows that AX recently it seems that the bank’s net interest margins have not been as stable compared to its competitors, therefore expect greater volatility within the NIMs.

While assessing the NIM standard deviation over the short term, Axos Financial recorded a trailing 12-month standard deviation while incorporating the most recent quarterly data of 0.0026300, ranking AX as the most volatile compared to similar banks. This reflects that the bank’s loan book has been less steady compared to its competitors, however one should note that this volatility has been to the upside.

Nonperforming Loans to Total Loans Ratio

As evident from the information below with the recent quarterly data included, over the previous ten years AX has witnessed their NPL ratio span between 1.20% and 0.40%. When we put this alongside the banking industry, Axos Financial from 2016 to the last twelve months outperformed the banking sector as a whole. This signals that Axos Financial, through the preceding ten years, has demonstrated nonperforming loans to total loans ratio that outperform the broader banking industry while at the same time outperforming in terms of net interest margins. This shows that the management team has done a stellar job at minimizing balance sheet risk while also delivering a strong upside on their loans. In the last year, we can see that AX has outperformed the banking industry. This is because the bank has shown strong lending discipline despite being able to drive a solid net interest margin in the last twelve months.

While contrasting AX’s ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio in relation to its competitors, it is clear that CUBI, FIBK, and ASB every one of them has a ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio (inclusive of the latest quarterly numbers) of 0.25%, 0.65%, and 0.55%; thus AX is positioned second within this assessment. In my view, since AX has a ten-year median NPL ratio of 0.55%, I think that AX is well positioned to achieve acceptable net interest margins given the low level of risk that their loan book presents.

Leverage

Axos Financial has achieved and maintained a ten-year median asset to equity ratio (with the most recent quarterly metrics factored in) of 10.3. From 2016 to the trailing twelve months, the data shows that Axos Financial’s assets to equity ratio has shown some variability, where in 2016 the assets to equity ratio was 11.12 and in the last twelve months the assets to equity ratio was 9.40. Through this period, the data shows that AX’s assets to equity ratio reached a peak of 11.25 in 2020, while also finding a low of 9.40 in TTM. Since 2022, we have seen the management team dial back loan book leverage because of the recent interest rate rises. In my perspective, the overall trend suggests that the bank has become less aggressive in the past decade in terms of leverage, which is a conservative approach that helps to de-risk the bank’s balance sheet, particularly given the current macro environment. In this quarter, the assets to equity ratio is currently 8.9% below the ten-year median assets to equity ratio.

In our observations, Axos Financial has the third-lowest degree of ten-year median leverage in comparison to its peers, and taking into account that AX also has a better than average nonperforming loans to total loans ratio, this indicates that the management team are capable of managing balance sheet risk while simultaneously maintaining driving higher than average returns on equity through higher levels of leverage as demonstrated by their assets to equity ratio.

In the latest quarter, Axos Financial achieved year-over-year growth of -9.6%, suggesting that in the near term, AX’s leverage has been in a downward indicating that the immediate strategy is to decrease the bank’s leverage, decreasing risk slightly but also positioning the bank to lower their ROE.

Efficiency Ratio

I think the efficiency ratio is a significant metric in indicating how effectively banks manage their operational expenses, and in the banking field, an efficiency ratio less than 60% is generally considered preferable. AX’s efficiency ratio from 2016 through to the present quarter has been below industry benchmarks, where the efficiency ratio in 2016 began at 34% and then worsened to 46%. We can also observe that the efficiency ratio bottomed out at 34% in 2016 and got as high as 51% in 2019. Since 2016, AX has achieved success in documenting a total of ten years throughout the last decade in which the efficiency ratio stayed below 60%, resulting in a median efficiency ratio over ten years of 47%. This shows that the bank's management team has done an excellent job in minimizing overhead expenses that would eat into net profit margins.

In the process of comparing AX’s ten-year median efficiency ratio in comparison to its counterparts, it is clear that CUBI, FIBK, and ASB all maintain a ten-year median nonperforming loans to total loans ratio of 57%, 61%, and 66%, where AX is ranked the lowest alongside its competing entities. As I see it, when set against the backdrop of other similar banks, the management team has demonstrated that they have done an effective job at managing overhead costs at the bank.

Deposits and Funding Sources

Axos Financial, over the last decade, has successfully kept a loan to deposit ratio of under 100% in only two of the past ten years. This shows that the bank is reliant on other more expensive funding sources, such as Federal Home Loan Bank Advances, to meet the demand of the loan book. I do believe that since the bank often relies on alternative funding sources, the management team should look to improve the deposits side of the business.

Focusing on the latest quarter, Axos Financial achieved a loan to deposit ratio of 101% compared to 104% in the same period of the prior year. This shows a 3.0% decrease in the loan to deposit ratio. Since the loan to deposit ratio this quarter is greater than 100%, this implies that Axos Financial's access to cheap fund sources will be problematic as the ratio is above 100%.

Return on Assets and Return on Equity

Axos Financial, over the past ten years, when including the latest quarterly numbers, has had a median return on assets of 1.7%, whilst achieving a ten-year median return on equity of 17.1%. We can see that in 2016, both the ROA and ROE were 1.8% and 19.6%, respectively. We can also observe that ROA peaked at 2.1% in 2024 before returning to an ROA of 1.9% in the last twelve months, while the ROE reached a height of 21.4% in 2024 before seeing an ROE of 18.8% in the last twelve months.

When making a comparison of AX compared to its fellow banks across the recent ten-year span, it is clear that AX has the first highest rate of return on assets and the first greatest return on equity. It is important to consider that a higher return on equity is not always superior to a lower return on equity (although it usually is) as an astute analyst should consider how much extra risk (higher leverage or riskier loans) the bank took, if any to achieve the outsized returns on equity. Taking this into account, recognizing that return on assets and return on equity have trended higher during recent years versus previous years and since the executive team has effectively managed nonperforming loans to total loans ratio and loan book leverage, I consider the return on equity to be acceptable.

In the recent quarter, Axos Financial reported a ROA of 1.83% and a ROE of 17.56%, versus a ROA of 1.89% and a ROE of 19.81% in the year ago quarter, representing growth of -3.2% and -11.4%, respectively. This ranks Axos Financial as having the best ROA YoY growth trend and best ROE YoY growth trend amongst their peers. Given the negative recent growth trend and the competitive ranking amongst other comparable banks, I believe the management has shown effective ROE management despite negative YoY growth, though investors should remain cautious about this trend.

Valuation

In the process of determining the valuation for Axos Financial, I’ve made the decision to set the following conditions:

  • A normalised return on assets of 1.7% which is the ten-year median that encompasses a variety of market conditions.
  • A normalized assets to equity ratio of 10.3 constituting the ten-year median assets to equity ratio.
  • A normalized ten-year median payout ratio of 0%.
  • A current price to book ratio of 1.50 and a projected five-year future price to book ratio of 1.57, which is calculated using the ten-year median.

As evident from the above, I anticipate that Axos Financial throughout the following five years will offer shareholders a CAGR of 18.0% of which 17.0% is expected from book value growth, 0.9% is predicted from multiple expansion and an anticipated yearly dividend yield of 0.0% which is dependent upon the earlier outlined payout ratio. Hence, I think that Axos Financial is currently a buy when accounting for the current share price of $65.38.

Conclusion

Axos Financial has a solid history when considering the net interest margin, ranking as the first highest amongst similar banks over the past decade at 4.2%. This exemplifies the fact that the bank can achieve above-average spreads between what is received in internet income versus what is paid out to customers in interest expenses. Despite the bank delivering strong net interest margins, I am impressed that the bank has been able to keep nonperforming loans low, demonstrating skillful risk management while using moderate leverage. When it comes to the efficiency ratio, Axos Financial stands out by surpassing industry norms, demonstrating control over overhead costs. Data from the most recent quarter reflects an efficiency ratio of 46%, putting AX below the efficiency ratio benchmark of 60%. In addition, the management team has been able to drive strong ROA and ROE, showcasing their ability to increase the bank’s intrinsic value over time. Furthermore, in the previous 12 months, ROE has undergone a negative growth rate. When analyzing this bank, alongside my valuation, I forecast an expected yearly return of 18.0%, making AX a buy.
